title: Discipline
summary:
We must all suffer from one of two pains: the pain of discipline or the pain of regret.
- Jim Rohn
content:
I greatly admire anyone who has discipline, for that I have found how hard it really is to be.

I have suffered tremendously over my life by being weak. Being weak is basically not being disciplined. Being weak is seeking comfort when you shouldn't seek it. Being weak is not being true to your words. Being weak is ignoring those precise moments in your life where you know that you shouldn't do something, but you still end up doing it.

I am weak. It's not easy to be strong and I feel really glad. Because it shows that there are no shortcuts to have a disciplined life with high integrity. Being disciplined is basically being unaffected by pain or pleasure. You don't run towards pleasure, nor do you run away from pain. You are just focused on your goal and choose the optimal path without considering how you feel, because, for you, pain and pleasure are the same. You realize that both arise from the same root and basically are the two sides of the same coin.

Discipline is freedom. Freedom from your mind. Freedom from the attachments. Freedom from the natural way of life. Your get to choose your life independent from what nature provides you in the form of pain or pleasure. You are a different beast.

title: We Need a Better Approach for Safety Evaluations
summary: Safety evaluations for both open and closed-source models must be conducted by an independent organization, and this should be a requirement, not a choice.
content:
Today, each lab does capabilities and safety tests on its models before release.

Last year, OpenAI and Anthropic went further. Each lab ran its own safety tests on the other lab's models [1]. This is great. More independent organizations testing will release more vulnerabilities and increase assurance. But this was a choice, and it only happened once. There is currently no incentive to do this: if one lab finds severe risks, the other might need to shut down its model, or their model might look worse in the eyes of the public. However, they can optimize their model on their own safety evaluation scores, and for sure, there are many incentives to share what they want to share.

Each lab tests safety in its own way. There is no shared standard. And a lab that builds a model should not be the only one that grades it. When the stakes are this high, safety evaluations from the same lab are not enough.

I strongly believe we need one independent organization on top of all of this, which only does safety evaluations. It should be backed by many governments working together that mutually agree on the safety reports from this organization. Its only job is to evaluate how safe the models really are.

This organization would use one universal framework. That means the same core set of tests for every model, from every company. It would attack each model with different adversarial attacks.

A model could not go public until it passes the minimum requirements of that independent organization's safety evaluations. A new drug does not go on sale just because the maker says it is safe, so why should an AI model do so?

Open-source and closed-source models do not carry the same risks. So the framework needs two separate tracks.

Right now, open models face fewer safety checks than closed ones. But the capability gap is small. Open models are only about four months behind the best closed models [2]. And the closed models are already strong. The Virology Capabilities Test, run on closed models in 2024 and 2025, found they could answer hard virology lab questions at the level of a PhD expert in that field [3]. Today's frontier open models already beat those older closed models on many general benchmarks.

In an AIxBio Hackathon organized by Apart Research, my team sent moderate-risk biology questions to frontier closed and open models across five biosecurity areas [4]. We tested several jailbreak methods. Open models like DeepSeek V4 and Kimi K2.5 refused far less often than Claude Sonnet 4.6 and GPT-5.4, even before any attack.

If one independent group tested every model with the same hard, hidden set of attacks, there are three advantages.

First, the results would be comparable because every model faced the same test. Second, the grade would be more trustworthy because the tester does not make a profit from the lab. Third, the tests could stay secret and keep changing.

If the tests are public or somehow leaked, then a model can learn to pass the test without being truly safe. A hidden test, held by a neutral group and changed often, fills that gap.

I want to be honest. This is not easy. Firstly, global coordination in this AI race is unlikely in the current scenario. Similarly, scaling labs might not be interested in this idea as well, since it can increase the time window before new models are released, and also some safety risks might take a long time to fix, reducing their profits. Similarly, some of these test questions are dangerous knowledge by themselves. So the group holding them must be very secure. The tests must keep changing, or models will slowly learn their style. And the group would need real experts and deep access to the models to do the job well.

These are hard problems. But we can work on them. The labs are still grading themselves, deploying models of their choice, and sharing their responsible scaling policies by adhering when they want to. That is not safe.

---

References

  1. Findings from a pilot Anthropic–OpenAI alignment evaluation exercise. OpenAI (2025). Each lab ran its own internal evaluations on the other's models, and noted the results are not meant as exact, apples-to-apples comparisons. https://openai.com/index/openai-anthropic-safety-evaluation/

  2. Open models lag state-of-the-art closed models by 4 months. Edwards, J. and Emberson, L. — Epoch AI (2026). Since January 2026, the best open-weight models have trailed frontier closed models by about four months on the Epoch Capabilities Index. https://epoch.ai/data-insights/open-closed-eci-gap

  3. Virology Capabilities Test (VCT): A Multimodal Virology Q&A Benchmark. Götting, J. et al. (2025). arXiv:2504.16137. Expert virologists averaged 22.1% in their own sub-areas; OpenAI's o3 reached 43.8%, outperforming 94% of expert virologists. https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.16137

  4. BioRT-Bench: A Multi-Attack Red-Teaming Benchmark for Bio-Misuse Safeguards in Frontier LLMs. Paudel, A. et al. — Apart Research (2026). Multi-method red-teaming of frontier closed and open models on moderate-risk biosecurity queries across five categories. https://apartresearch.com/project/biortbench-a-multiattack-redteaming-benchmark-for-biomisuse-safeguards-in-frontier-llms-hgw0

title: Why I am so obsessed about AI Safety
summary: Some thoughts on why I chose AI Safety for my career
content:
What do I want to do in my life?

I came to the US as an undergrad in September 2024. Till that time, I still did not have any purpose in life whatsoever. I was influenced to go towards the common route most CS majors target, which is working as a software engineer. There were two particular reasons for me to pursue this:

  1. High Salary

  2. Passionate and curious people (basically an environment where I can be positively influenced and constantly improve myself)

I basically spent my whole freshman year on this. Building full-stack projects, solving LeetCode problems, connecting with people in the field, following zero2sudo and applying to literally every possible internship he shared and also in the common GitHub job posting websites. But still, I thought, "Is this really all? Is this all that I can do in my life?"

Common alternatives that came to my mind were: working in quant or my own startup (which basically gives much higher returns for both of my reasons to choose SWE). I talked with my friend, Hieu, who was aiming for the same things as me. We decided on going for quant rather than SWE. This desire didn't last long either.

I wondered. Would those two reasons suffice for me to have a fulfilling life? In my deathbed, what would I consider a fulfilling life? I knew income and the right environment matter a lot, but is that enough?

Certain things drove me in this moment. First was the EXTREME desire for greatness. My whole body was rushing with that craving to become exceptional, or to not be ordinary. Becoming exceptionally rich, healthy, strong, and in most aspects of life that society admires or at least would choose to have if they had a pill that could instantly take them there. Besides this, the desire to have a great life partner also influenced me a lot. Having a great partner is highly correlated with how great you really are.

But still, I thought, is this enough? Does all of this suffice? Does, for example, becoming a billionaire, with a great lifestyle, a great wife, and a great social life (⭐), suffice? At the end, that is all I can aspire to. What else can be there on a personal level?

The answer to this question was basically the quest for finding what would make me happy in the long term. It's not easy to know what would satisfy us the most in the long-term. But I knew for sure that the purpose had to be more than just "me" for it to be maximally fulfilling. How happy can I be by (⭐) if the world is just suffering? Not much, indeed.

This is what led me to AI Safety. I was influenced by a lot of things, but, in essence, it was a selfish reason. I thought ensuring humanity's transition through this technological adolescence, as Dario puts it, would fulfill me the most.

I calculated the impact I can make by the number of lives I can save. This is not the best way to calculate the impact, but it does its job. Assume AI causing human extinction in the next 100 years is 10%. This is a pretty fair estimate, especially considering Geoffrey Hinton and other AI researchers put it way above it in a much stricter timeline. Assume that there are 8 billion lives at that time, and my contribution can decrease the risks of AI causing a human-extinction-level incident by 0.1%. I think 0.1% is a fair estimate considering only ~1100 people are working full-time on AI Safety. Even with this, I would have 800,000 lives of impact. If I work for 10,000 hours, that's 80 lives every hour and more than 1 life each minute. I can happily sacrifice anything for such a high return.

That's what I am obsessed with: AI Safety.

Thank you everyone who brought me to this field and positively helped me. Couple of names:

Geoffrey Hinton, SPAR & AISC, Justin Shenk (My mentor for SPAR & AISC), BlueDot Impact, Hieu (my friend who chose to pursure AI Safety together), David (who helped me so much to upskill my leadership and communication abilities), 80000 hours, Rob Miles, Sandy (my AI Safety mentor), etc.

title: Tiers of AI Risk
summary: A simple framework for sorting AI risks by two things: how bad the worst case is, and whether we could ever recover from it.
content:
There are a lot of AI risks, and not all of them are of the same intensity. I sort them into tiers using just two questions. The first is severity: how bad is the worst likely outcome? The second is reversibility: if it happens, can we recover, or is the damage permanent?

I plot every risk on those two axes and three tiers come out:

Reversibility →
MinorSeverityEnds humanity
Tier 1
Tier 2
Tier 3
Bio threats
s‑risks
Misalignment
CBRN uplift
Power concentration
Gradual disempowerment
Cyberattacks
Environmental
Eroded truth
Governance gap
Unemployment
Lost meaning
Bias
Privacy
Skill loss
IP harms
AI welfare
← Irreversible (no recovery)Recoverable →
Tier 1 · existential Tier 2 · recoverable Tier 3 · correctable ◯ hollow = speculative / off‑axis

One thing to note: irreversible by itself is not enough for Tier 1. Some environmental damage is irreversible: an extinct species does not come back, but humanity survives it, so it sits in Tier 2. The Tier 1 line is specifically about whether the worst case ends humanity or ends our control over our own future.

The three tiers

Click any tier to open it, then click a risk inside for the detail.

Tier 1Existential Worst case is the permanent end of humanity, or of our ability to steer our own future. Irreversible. These dominate the calculation: if an option clearly raises a Tier 1 risk, no amount of normal benefit makes up for it.
AI‑enabled biological threats

I think this is the most severe risk right now. In an Apart Research hackathon, my team found that frontier open‑source models are easy to jailbreak even without fine‑tuning. Once jailbroken, they can greatly uplift an intermediate‑level person with wet‑lab access toward creating a novel, dangerous pathogen. The reason bio stays in Tier 1 is the asymmetry: defense is hard, attack is getting cheaper, and a single engineered pandemic could be civilization‑ending and impossible to reverse once it is out. This is the risk where the capability is already real, not just theory.

Loss of control / misalignment

Not being able to understand a model's true intentions becomes critical as systems get more capable, especially at the stage of recursive self‑improvement, where a model improves itself faster than we can check it. If we can't tell whether a powerful system is actually pursuing the goals we wanted, we can lose the ability to correct it, through deceptive alignment (behaving well only when watched), scheming, and power‑seeking. The worst case is permanent loss of human control.

Power concentration and lock‑in

AI changes the old balance of power. In the past, even authoritarian rulers needed many cooperating humans to run an economy and a military, and those people could refuse, resist, or revolt. If AI can do most of that work, whoever controls the most powerful systems no longer needs broad cooperation to stay in power. The danger is a permanent lock‑in: one state, company, or person taking control with no real way for anyone to check or reverse it. Normal power eventually fades; AI‑backed power might not.

Gradual disempowerment

The quieter version of power concentration. Instead of one group taking control, humanity slowly gives it up, one reasonable decision at a time. Each step makes sense (handing a task to AI is faster, cheaper, better), and competition rewards whoever delegates more. But over time the systems running the economy, government, and security become ones we don't understand and can't override, and the feedback loops that kept institutions responsive get weaker. No single disaster, no misaligned AI needed. Just a slow drift until humans can't really steer. The end result is still the permanent loss of human control.

Other CBRN uplift (chemical, radiological, nuclear)

The same logic as bio (AI lowering the barrier to weapons of mass destruction) applies to chemical, radiological, and nuclear domains. I rank bio highest because the uplift there feels closest, but severe CBRN uplift belongs in Tier 1 for the same reason.

Governance failure

When institutions can't keep up with AI, you get a gap where there are no strong rules. The gap itself is recoverable (the rules can always be written later), so by the strict reversibility test it looks like Tier 2. I keep it in Tier 1 anyway, because the range of Tier 1 risks it directly feeds is so wide. Without strong governance, the labs racing to scale have far less incentive to act for the general good; a company that values short‑term profit over long‑term wellbeing will under‑invest in safety, and that one gap quietly raises bio, misalignment, and power‑concentration risk all at once. The gap can be closed, but while it is open it lifts the risks that cannot.

A note on suffering risks (s‑risks)

Some people include "s‑risks" (outcomes with huge amounts of suffering that may be worse than extinction) at this tier or beyond. I mention them to be complete. They are more speculative, but they fit the Tier 1 logic of irreversible, civilization‑scale harm.

Tier 2Large‑scale, but recoverable Badly damages how society works, but leaves humanity alive and able to recover over time. Serious, and worth real attention, but they don't end the game. We can make laws, build institutions, and adapt.
Mass unemployment and job displacement

If AI automates a large share of work faster than the economy can adjust, the result is mass unemployment, inequality, and instability. Dangerous, but recoverable: societies can redistribute, retrain, and restructure. Humanity survives and can still respond.

Erosion of shared truth

AI‑generated misinformation and synthetic media at scale can damage a society's ability to agree on basic facts. This feeds political instability and makes group decisions harder and makes every other problem harder to solve, but a society can in principle rebuild trustworthy information channels.

Loss of meaning and purpose

If AI does most of the things people used to find meaning in (work, creating, discovery), many may face a real crisis of purpose. A real harm at scale, but something humanity can adapt to over time.

Large‑scale cyberattacks

AI‑enabled attacks on critical infrastructure can cause huge damage. I put the normal version in Tier 2 because systems can be rebuilt and hardened. But an attack bad enough to collapse the systems society depends on with no recovery would move toward Tier 1.

Environmental impact

The energy and water demands of AI data centers carry real environmental costs. Some of this damage is irreversible (lost species, emitted carbon), but humanity survives it, so by my definition it sits in Tier 2 even though it is irreversible.

Tier 3Real, but correctable Everything else: real harms that are smaller and easier to fix. Worth fixing, but they don't threaten the foundations of society.
Privacy and data leakage

Serious for individuals, but addressable with law and engineering.

Bias and discrimination

Real harm, but identifiable and correctable with effort. When bias gets big enough to lock in structural injustice, it can move up toward Tier 2.

IP and creator harms

Using creators' work without permission is a real fairness problem, but it is about compensation and law, not survival.

Over‑reliance and skill loss

As we depend on AI, our own skills fade. Worth watching, but reversible at the individual level.

AI welfare

Whether AI systems themselves have moral status. I list it here because it is real and growing, even though it sits on a different axis from the rest.

Things the tiers alone don't show

A clean three‑tier list hides a few things that matter when you actually use the framework.

Risk amplifiers

Some things don't belong to a single tier; they make every tier worse. The biggest is race dynamics: the pressure between labs and countries to deploy faster, which cuts safety everywhere. Anything that speeds up the race raises everyone's tail risk.

Risks are connected

The tiers are not independent. Accelerating AI in science partly drives the bio risk and the timeline risk at the same time. When you raise one Tier 1 risk, you often raise others, so the real danger is bigger than scoring each one alone would suggest.

Some fixes trade one tier risk for another

This is the tricky one. Restricting access to cutting‑edge AI chips reduces proliferation: it stops many bad actors from training powerful models, lowering Tier 1 misuse risk. But the same restriction concentrates capability in the few actors allowed to have the chips, which slightly raises Tier 1 power‑concentration risk. So a good intervention is not one that lowers risk on every axis; it is one whose net effect across the tiers is positive. The chip case is the clearest example of why I name power concentration directly: it is the risk that several well‑meaning "safety" moves quietly increase.

Proliferation and concentration are opposites

Cheaper compute and looser chip access push toward proliferation, where power spreads to many actors, including bad ones. Restriction pushes toward concentration, where power sits with a few. Both extremes carry Tier 1 risk, just of different kinds. The goal is the narrow middle, not either end.

title: Thinking About Alignment
summary: Some initial thoughts on what it means to align AI systems with human values, and why the problem is harder than it first appears.
content:
The alignment problem is often framed simply: make AI do what we want. But when you start to unpack that statement, layers of difficulty emerge that make the problem one of the most important challenges of our time.

Why Values Are Hard to Specify

Consider a simple example: we might ask an AI to "maximize human happiness." On the surface this sounds reasonable. But happiness is not a single, measurable quantity. It varies across cultures, individuals, and time. An AI system that takes this objective literally might find shortcuts that satisfy the letter of the goal while violating its spirit, a phenomenon researchers call specification gaming.

The Role of Interpretability

One promising approach to alignment is to build tools that let us understand what AI systems are actually doing internally. If we can inspect a model's reasoning process, we're better equipped to catch misalignment before it causes harm. This is why I believe interpretability research is foundational to AI safety.

Looking Forward

We're at an early stage, and the field is moving fast. I think the most important thing right now is to build a community of researchers who take these problems seriously and develop both the theoretical frameworks and practical tools needed to ensure AI systems remain aligned with human values as they become more capable.
